schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads the reign and ruin of Hoshea, who forced his way to the crown by treason yet gained possession only seven or eight years later, the delay a just chastisement fulfilling Hosea 10:3.
Gill resolves the twelfth-year date by an interregnum, or by Hoshea's being a vassal not reckoned to reign until he rebelled, then nine years, four under Ahaz and five under Hezekiah.
Wesley dates the usurpation to Ahaz's fourth year but the settled reign to the twelfth, counting the nine years from that confirmation; Clarke's school shares the concurrent-reckoning approach.
Ellicott reckons from the surrounding reigns that Hoshea succeeded Pekah in the fourteenth year of Ahaz, mounting the throne as an Assyrian vassal per Tiglath-Pileser's inscription.

debates.question: How is Hoshea's accession in the twelfth year of Ahaz reconciled with his slaying Pekah in the fourth?

sides.position: an interregnum, or Hoshea unacknowledged as king until he cast off vassalage, dates the reign from the twelfth year
the regnal numbers place his accession in the fourteenth year of Ahaz, where he began as an Assyrian vassal

schools.summary: Gill takes 'not as the kings before him' concretely: Hoshea worshipped no Baal and could not worship the calves, already carried off, and removed the border-garrisons that had barred the way to Jerusalem.
Ellicott cautions we can hardly assume Hoshea abandoned the calf of Bethel, though he may have discountenanced the Baals and Asheras; the last kings of a falling line are not always the worst.
Wesley agrees Hoshea neither worshipped Baal nor compelled the calf-worship, yet the measure of Israel's sins being full, vengeance comes without remedy.

themes: not as the kings before him
judgment on a comparatively better king

schools.summary: Gill takes Shalmaneser for the son of Tiglath-Pileser, whose name Josephus found in the Tyrian archives and Metasthenes gave a seventeen-year reign.
Clarke identifies him as the son and successor of Tiglath-Pileser, the Shalman of Hosea 10:14; Wesley notes the same, and that the presents made Hoshea tributary.
Ellicott, from the cuneiform record, fixes him as Shalmaneser IV (727-722 B.C.), predecessor of Sargon, whose Tyre campaign (per Menander) ran on into Sargon's reign.

debates.question: Who was Shalmaneser king of Assyria?

sides.position: the son and successor of Tiglath-Pileser
the same person as the later Sennacherib
holders: the ancient Hebrew writers (as reported by Wesley)

schools.summary: Gill reads the conspiracy as a scheme of revolt detected before it ripened, the Assyrian learning of it through spies.
Clarke states the plot plainly: Hoshea sought to shake off the yoke by a treaty with So of Egypt and ceased the annual tribute.

Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch labour over 'So' (Sabako/Sevechus) and 'conspiracy' (rejecting Thenius's 'falsehood'), and note Hoshea was imprisoned before the siege, since Sargon never mentions capturing the king.

sides.position: one of the two Shebeks of the 25th Ethiopian dynasty, Sevechus or his father Sabako
Shabaka, the Greek Sabaco, first king of the Ethiopian dynasty, whom Sargon defeated at Raphia

schools.summary: Gill notes the invasion was unresisted, Hoshea not daring to come out and fight.
Clarke reads the three-year siege as proof Samaria was well fortified, provisioned, and defended.

Keil and Delitzsch date the fall to Hoshea's ninth year (722/721 B.C.) and locate the exile in Assyria and Media; Ellicott observes the text does not say Shalmaneser himself 'took' the city, leaving room for Sargon.

themes: the fall in Hoshea's ninth year

schools.summary: Ellicott anchors the capture in Sargon's Botta inscription (27,280 men carried off, 50 chariots) and locates Halah, Habor, and Gozan from the Assyrian lists.
Clarke sees the fall foretold in Hosea 13:16 and Micah 1:6 and dates the kingdom at 254 years; Wesley mourns the tribes mingled and lost, a remnant escaping into Judah.
Matthew Henry reads the deportation as the exile of a people who forgot God and were themselves forgotten, buried among the nations.
Gill reckons the ninth year the last of the reign, dated from full authority or from the casting off of the yoke.

debates.question: What became of the deported ten tribes?

sides.position: absorbed and effaced among the nations, only a remnant escaping into Judah
surviving and traceable, the Afghans being their descendants

outliers.note: Devotes a long note, citing Sir William Jones, to arguing that the Afghans are 'by far the most probable' descendants of the lost ten tribes.

schools.summary: Matthew Henry traces the ruin not to second causes but to the First Cause: the LORD removed Israel, the Assyrian only the rod of his anger (Isaiah 10:5), yet by necessary justice, for they procured it themselves.

Keil and Delitzsch read 'when', not 'because', with the apodosis delayed, verses 7-17 explaining Israel's sin from the redemption out of Egypt; Ellicott grounds Jehovah's exclusive claim in the Exodus preamble (Exodus 20:2).
Gill names the sin here enlarged upon as the cause of the captivity in a word: idolatry.
Gill marks the aggravation: they walked in the ways of the Canaanites whose ejection should have warned them, expressly cautioned against these customs in Leviticus 18:3.

Keil and Ellicott draw out the twofold guilt: the statutes of the dispossessed nations, and the calf-worship the kings of Israel had made — idolatry proper, and a heathenish worship of Jehovah under the form of a bullock.
Gill takes 'did secretly' of idolatry done in private, from shame or from an atheistical notion that God does not see, or as 'covered' under thin pretences.
Clarke finds much hidden and universal idolatry, from watch-tower to fenced city; Wesley allows both the shameful secret rites and the calf-worship 'covered' with pretences of necessity.

Keil (with Hengstenberg) reads 'they covered words not right concerning Jehovah', concealing his true nature by perverting his word, rejecting Thenius and Gesenius; Ellicott agrees the sense is 'covered', a verb of perfidy.

debates.question: What does the rare verb rendered 'did secretly' mean?

sides.position: they practised their idolatry secretly, in private
they covered and concealed the true nature of Jehovah by perverting his word
they 'trifled' (Thenius) or 'acted perfidiously' (Gesenius)
holders: Thenius and Gesenius (as reported and rejected by Keil and Delitzsch)

themes: tower of the watchmen to the fenced city

schools.summary: Gill takes the objects for idols of stone and wood set in shaded spots, comparing the idol-strewn fields and groves of the Indians of his own day.
Keil sees monuments and asherim on every hill, condemned since Jeroboam (1 Kings 14:23) and charged against Judah too; Ellicott renders 'pillars and Asheras', the second degree of guilt.
Clarke identifies the images of various idols and the places set apart for the rites of Ashtaroth or Venus.

themes: every high hill and green tree

schools.summary: Gill notes even Judah burned incense on the high places, so the sin was not the north's alone.

Keil marks the dreadful irony that the word for the Canaanites' expulsion means 'to lead into exile', the very fate now overtaking Israel; Ellicott presses 'wicked things' to include the hideous immoralities of Canaan's nature-worship.
Gill hears the scorn in 'dunghill gods', recalling the pagan Sterculius, so debased were the deities Israel served.
Keil renders 'they served the clods' (gillulim), masses of stone worshipped as gods against Exodus 20:3; Ellicott corrects to 'the dunglings', a term of contempt.
Gill catalogues the messengers — Ahijah, Elijah and Elisha, Hosea, Amos, Micah — by whom the LORD testified and called them to repent.
Clarke makes the succession of prophets the aggravation of their guilt; Wesley describes the seers as those to whom God revealed his mind by visions, bearing witness by miracles.

Keil says God was not content with the law's prohibitions but bore witness through all his prophets, Judah named to signal her coming fate; Ellicott renders 'adjured' and defines the seers as emergency messengers of sudden impulse.

debates.question: Why are Israel and Judah named together here?

sides.position: because both apostatised, and Judah was already preparing the same fate for herself
because the reference is to the time before the kingdom was divided
holders: Thenius (as reported and rejected by Keil and Delitzsch)

schools.summary: Gill states plainly they would not hear or obey the prophets' admonitions.
Wesley draws the figure of stubborn oxen that will not bow the neck to the yoke of God's precepts.

Ellicott insists the 'unbelief' is moral not intellectual, an unbelief showing itself as disobedience; Keil carries it into the fathers' rejection of the covenant and 'following vanity' (Jeremiah 2:5).
Gill fixes the rejected covenant as the one made at Sinai and Horeb, flung away.
Ellicott parses 'vanity' (hebel) as a breath, denoting the idols, and 'became vain' as dealing foolishly, answering to Romans 1:21.
Wesley presses the moral logic: by worshipping idols of nothingness the people were made like them, vain, sottish, and senseless.
Gill reads 'left all the commandments' with James 2:10 — he that offends in one point is guilty of all.
Keil unfolds the climax — two calves (1 Kings 12:28), the Asherah, the host of heaven, and Baal; Ellicott, with Schlottmann, identifies the Asherah as Ashtoreth, a real sacred tree beside Baal.
Wesley reads a descent by degrees from partial to total apostasy, glossing the host of heaven as the stars.

themes: the host of heaven and Baal

schools.summary: Gill reads the passing through the fire as an offering to Baal or Moloch, the sun in the element of fire, by lustration or by burning.
Ellicott names the Moloch cult (2 Kings 16:3), the forbidden arts (Deuteronomy 18:10), and idolatry as servitude; Keil carries it to the anger of God that removed Israel, leaving only Judah.
Clarke reads 'sold themselves' as being abandoned to the devil, to work all iniquity with greediness.
Gill fixes on the cause of the wrath, nothing being more provoking to God than idolatry.
Clarke unfolds the removal as banishment from land, temple, and ordinance; Clarke and Wesley reckon under 'Judah' also Benjamin, Levi, and the godly who joined her.
Ellicott takes 'removed out of his sight' as banishment from the land and 'the tribe' as the kingdom (1 Kings 11:36); Keil reads the verse as the long-delayed apodosis of the indictment.
Gill reads Judah's sin as both infection and aggravation — drawn into idolatry by the north's example, persisting even after seeing its ruin.
Keil takes the verse as a parenthesis on Judah's high-place and idol worship under many kings; Ellicott calls Judah no real exception, comparing the 'statutes of Omri' (Micah 6:16).
Wesley remembers Judah's wickedness as an aggravation of Israel's, whose evil example instructed and provoked her neighbour.

themes: the statutes of Israel and Omri

schools.summary: Gill reads the rejection as a divorce — the LORD wrote 'Lo-ammi' on the ten tribes and gave them a bill of divorcement.

Ellicott takes 'Israel' in the narrow sense, the spoilers being Syrians and Assyrians (Judges 2:14); Keil takes 'all the seed of Israel' of the whole nation, and distinguishes chastening rejection from final exile.
Wesley reads 'all Israel' of all the tribes successively, though not every individual, since many had removed into Judah.

debates.question: How wide is 'all the seed of Israel'?

sides.position: the ten tribes only, in the narrow sense of these verses
not merely the ten tribes but the whole nation

schools.summary: Gill refers the rending to Rehoboam's day, the kingdom torn into twelve pieces with ten given to Jeroboam, ascribed to the LORD's purpose and providence.
Keil argues Jehovah rent Israel from David's house (1 Kings 11:31); Ellicott reads the verb as 'Israel rent [itself]', favouring Thenius's restored passive.
Wesley calls the people's making Jeroboam king a sin, and the calf-worship 'a great sin' so named to meet their conceit that it was small.

debates.question: Who is the subject of 'he rent Israel from the house of David'?

sides.position: Jehovah rent Israel from the house of David in judgment
Israel rent itself away, the verb to be restored as a passive

schools.summary: Gill fastens on the twofold obedience Israel gave Jeroboam — keeping his ban on Jerusalem and worshipping his calves.

Keil traces the persistence to the removal threatened by all the prophets from Jeroboam onward, with a careful note that 'unto this day' proves nothing of a return; Ellicott sums the obstinate persistence in a sentence.
Gill glosses the removal plainly as the captivity into Assyria, the just term of all before.

Ellicott stresses the fulfilment 'as he said by the prophets', quotes Robertson Smith that the lost-tribes problem is 'purely fanciful', and notes the land not wholly emptied (2 Chronicles 30:1); Keil takes the resettling king to be a later monarch than Shalmaneser (Ezra 4:2).

themes: the lost tribes a fanciful problem

schools.summary: Gill weighs whether the king who resettled Samaria was Shalmaneser or rather Esarhaddon (Ezra 4:2).
Matthew Henry draws the lesson that the holy land became heathen again — 'see what work sin makes' — the surplus of Assyria transplanted to possess it.
Clarke explains the Assyrian policy of substituting peoples to cut off insurrection, and identifies Cuthah with Cush and Ava with the Avim (Deuteronomy 2:23).
Ellicott anchors the settlement in Sargon's inscriptions (Babylonians in year 1, Arabs in year 7), with Esarhaddon reinforcing later; Keil, with Gill, inclines to Esarhaddon from Ezra 4:2.

debates.question: Which king of Assyria resettled Samaria with foreign colonists?

sides.position: Esarhaddon, on the evidence of Ezra 4:2
Sargon settled the first colonists, Esarhaddon only reinforcing later

themes: colonists from Babylon, Cuthah, and Hamath

schools.summary: Gill marks the strangeness that the settlers did not even add the God of the land to their own gods, as heathen usually did.
Clarke reasons the emptied land bred lions naturally, yet holds God likely did send them as a scourge; Wesley presses that God asserted his sovereignty over the land.
Ellicott sees the lions multiplied naturally but taken and endorsed as a divine token (Leviticus 26:22); Keil is firmer that they were sent by Jehovah as a punishment.

debates.question: Were the lions a natural consequence or a supernatural judgment?

sides.position: lions multiplied naturally in the emptied land, understood by the colonists as the local deity's wrath
sent by Jehovah as a punishment, per Leviticus 26:22

schools.summary: Gill notes the representation was made in letters or by messengers.
Clarke glosses mishpat, 'judgment', as the way the God of the land is to be worshipped.

Ellicott takes mishpat as the appointed cultus (cf. the Koran's 'din') and reads 'men spake', the prefects; Keil sets the petition as the spring of the coming syncretism, the settlers asking for a priest.
Matthew Henry draws the irony that the heathen, calling him 'the God of the land', shamed Israel by seeking the very worship Israel had despised.

themes: the manner of the God of the land

schools.summary: Gill notes only that these were the king's orders in reply.
Ellicott corrects the plural to 'let him go and dwell' after the Syriac and Vulgate, and renders 'cause to go' and 'carried away'.
Clarke looks to the long outcome: the imperfect teaching in the end overthrew the idolatry, leaving the Samaritans monotheists, now a remnant of some thirty families.

themes: the later purging of Samaritan idolatry

schools.summary: Gill gathers the traditions naming the priest (Uzziah, or Esdras per Epiphanius) and is sure he was one of the priests of the calves, settling in Bethel.
Ellicott renders 'was teaching', a settled work, fixes on Bethel as the mark of a calf-priest, and cautions 'fear the LORD' is ceremonial not ethical.
Wesley infers from the priest's origin, his residence at Bethel, and his manner of making priests that he taught the worship as practised in Israel.
Gill reads the plain persistence of their imported idolatry, each nation setting its own gods in the high places the Israelites had built.
Clarke notes every nation made gods after the fashion of those they had worshipped in their own country.
Ellicott presses that they did not fear Jehovah monotheistically but added his cultus to their ancestral deities, 'the Samaritans' meaning the people of northern Israel.
Gill canvasses Succoth-benoth as a hen and chickens, or the Pleiades, or the 'booths of the daughters' of Babylonian temple-prostitution (Herodotus).
Clarke reads Succoth-benoth as the tabernacles of the daughters (Melitta), Nergal as the solar orb (a cock), Ashima as fire (a goat).
Ellicott restores the deities from the monuments: Zarpanitum consort of Merodach, Nergal the lion-god of Kutha, and an Ashima of which nothing certain is known.

debates.question: How are the idols of the colonists to be identified?

sides.position: by the animal emblems of rabbinic tradition — hen, cock, goat
as known Babylonian and Assyrian deities restored from the monuments

schools.summary: Gill gives the rabbinic dog-form of Nibhaz and takes Tartak for a chain, the fixed stars, though the rabbis made it an ass.

Keil reports the rabbinic dog and ass but notes Gesenius's demons, and reads Adrammelech and Anammelech as Moloch-kin (2 Kings 19:37); Ellicott calls Nibhaz and Tartak unknown but restores Adar-malik and Anum-malik.
Clarke identifies Nibhaz with the Egyptian Anubis, Tartak with the ass or sun's chariot, and Anammelech with the Moloch of the Ammonites.

debates.question: What were Nibhaz, Tartak, and the Sepharvite gods?

sides.position: the rabbinic forms — Nibhaz a dog, Tartak an ass
Nibhaz and Tartak unknown, but Adrammelech and Anammelech the Assyrian Adar-malik and Anum-malik

schools.summary: Gill reads the opening as their compliance with the Bethel priest's instruction.
Clarke takes 'the lowest of them' of the meanest people, one priest not sufficing for so mixed a population; Wesley notes these priests sacrificed to the true God, needing no help for their own.
Ellicott corrects to 'of all orders / promiscuously', a mark of Jeroboam's mode restored (1 Kings 12:31); Keil reads the same, priests made from the mass of the people.

debates.question: What does 'of the lowest of them' mean?

sides.position: of the meanest sort of the people
of all orders, promiscuously, as in Jeroboam's ordinance

schools.summary: Gill puts the verdict in the fewest words — they worshipped both.

Clarke exposes the divided heart: fear of Jehovah, who sent the lions, prompting a prudential worship, while love of the idols gratified their passions; Wesley notes every nation kept its own gods besides.
Ellicott renders 'Jehovah were they fearing, and their own gods were they serving', a recapitulation of vv.28-32; Keil treats the verse as the summing-up of the whole mixed cultus.

themes: feared the LORD and served their own gods
fear for safety, love for idols

schools.summary: Gill offers three possible referents for 'they' — the colonists, the exiles unreformed, or the remnant left in the land.

Ellicott resolves the crux by a right fear versus a mongrel one, thinking of the remnant amalgamated with the settlers (John 4:12); Keil reads the mixed cultus as no true fear of the God of Jacob (1 Kings 18:31).
Wesley holds that though they pretended to fear both, in truth they feared neither, God rejecting the mongrel worship, their constant breach of his statutes the proof.

debates.question: How can v.33 say they 'feared the LORD' and v.34 that 'they fear not the LORD', and who is meant?

sides.position: the colonists, whose mixed worship is no true fear of the LORD in the sense of the Torah
the remnant of the ten tribes who amalgamated with the settlers

themes: a divided worship is no fear of God
the remnant of the ten tribes
worship not according to the Torah

schools.summary: Gill fixes the covenant recalled as the one made at Sinai, binding Israel to God alone.

Keil reads vv.35-39 as one connected charge — the fourfold prohibition, the sole worship of the God of the Exodus, the statutes kept for ever, and the promise of deliverance — which v.40 records Israel refusing.
Gill marks the Exodus named to press the obligation of gratitude, that the God who saved them might have their whole service.
Clarke finds three things in the verse — the object of worship (the self-existent LORD), its reason (the irresistible redemption from Egypt), and its manner (fear, humility, and sacrifice).

themes: true worship in fear, humility, and sacrifice

schools.summary: Gill glosses the statutes as those written on the two tables of stone, given by the finger of God to endure.
Ellicott had observed that this fourfold formula — statutes, ordinances, law, commandment — recurs as a fixed covenant expression, the pairs exegetical of one another.
Gill identifies the covenant not to be forgotten as the Sinai law, whose first table forbade the worship of any other god.
Ellicott marks that 'neither shall ye fear other gods' is repeated three times (vv.35, 37, 38) as the main point of the covenant between Jehovah and Israel.
Gill takes 'fear' in its full compass, the worship of God both inwardly and outwardly according to his revealed will.
Ellicott catches the emphatic pronoun — 'and He, on his part, will deliver you' — binding the fear required to the deliverance promised.
Gill reads the verdict without softening: they did not repent but persisted in their idolatries, even in captivity or in the land left behind.
Ellicott fixes 'they' as the Ephraimites, who continued clinging to the old-established cultus of the calves.
Gill draws the exact parallel: the nations feared the LORD and served their images just as Israel had served the Lord and the calves, and worshipped God and Baal together.
Clarke takes 'unto this day' to mean the note was written before the Babylonish captivity, after which none of Israel ever lapsed into idolatry.
Ellicott calls the verse a variation of v.33 and dates it two generations after 721 B.C., in the age of the last redactor; Keil lets it stand as the plain summary of the divided worship.

debates.question: What does 'unto this day' imply about the composition of Kings?

sides.position: the note was written before the Babylonish captivity of Judah
it was written two generations after the fall of Samaria, in the age of the last redactor (the exile of Judah)

themes: feared the LORD and served graven images
the fall of Israel as a monument
